context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 5

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 18th March, 2024
Days Covered: 08, 09, and 10 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Fibonacci Divisibility [13 points]

Recall the Fibonacci numbers (F0, F1, F2, ...) = (0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, ...), where Fn equals F_{n−1} + F_{n−2} for every n ≥ 2. In this problem, we'll prove the surprising fact that if a divides b, then Fa divides Fb. For example, F6 = 8 is a factor of F12 = 144.
Let a > 0 be a fixed (but unspecified) integer.

(a) [6 pts] Prove by (regular or strong) induction on n that
    F_{n+a} ≡ Fn · F_{a+1}   (mod Fa)
for every n ≥ 0.
In other words, when looking modulo Fa, the subsequence (Fa, F_{a+1}, F_{a+2}, ...) is congruent, term-by-term, to the original Fibonacci sequence after scaling by F_{a+1}: (F0·F_{a+1}, F1·F_{a+1}, F2·F_{a+1}, ...).
(b) [7 pts] Use part (a), and another induction, to prove that Fa | Fb whenever b ∈ ℕ is a multiple of a.
    Hint: What will you induct on?


Problem 2. Computing Modular Inverses [10 points]

Let's see a few common techniques for computing modular inverses! Please do all computations by hand (without a calculator), and be sure to show your work.

(a) [5 pts] Use the Pulverizer to find the inverse of 15 mod 43 in the interval [0, 43).
(b) [5 pts] Use Fermat's Little Theorem to find the inverse of 15 mod 43 in [0, 43).


Problem 3. Primality Testing [12 points]

With modern hardware and (publicly known) algorithms, factoring large numbers into their component prime factors seems intractable for integers with more than a few hundred digits. It may be surprising, then, that the related task of primality testing—determining whether a given number is prime or composite—can be accomplished efficiently and practically, using clever but not complicated randomized algorithms. Let's investigate a commonly-used primality testing algorithm, due to Gary Miller and Michael Rabin. For this problem, let n be the integer we wish to test.

(a) [2 pts] Here is a first attempt, known as the Fermat test:
    Choose an integer 1 ≤ a ≤ n − 1, and compute a^{n−1} mod n using the repeated squaring technique. If this result is not 1, return Composite. Otherwise, return I don't know.
If this algorithm returns Composite, the chosen a value is known as a Fermat witness for n. Prove that a Fermat witness is enough to prove that n is composite. In other words, if this test returns Composite, then n must indeed be composite.
    Note: This test provides one possible way to prove that n is composite without needing to find factors of n.

Some composite numbers can "fool" the Fermat test for too many values of a. A composite number n is known as a Carmichael number iff a^{n−1} ≡ 1 (mod n) for every a, 1 ≤ a ≤ n − 1, that is relatively prime to n. It is known that infinitely many Carmichael numbers exist. The three smallest are 561 = 3·11·17, 1105 = 5·13·17, and 1729 = 7·13·19.

(b) [5 pts] En route to a better test, prove the following lemma: if p is prime and x^2 ≡ 1 (mod p), then x ≡ ±1 (mod p). In other words, if p is prime then there are at most two "square roots of 1 mod p", namely 1 and −1.
    Hint: Use Lemma 9.4.2 in the textbook.

(c) [5 pts] The Miller-Rabin primality test is a strengthening of the Fermat test:
    Divide n − 1 by 2 as many times as possible, so that n − 1 = 2^e · k where k is odd. Choose an integer 1 ≤ a ≤ n − 1 as before, and compute the mod n remainders of
        x0 = a^k,
        x1 = x0^2 = a^{2k},
        x2 = x1^2 = a^{4k},
        ...
        xe = x_{e−1}^2 = a^{2^e·k} = a^{n−1}.
    If xe ≢ 1 (mod n), or if there is a pair of consecutive terms (xi, x_{i+1}) where x_{i+1} ≡ 1 (mod n) but xi ≢ ±1 (mod n), return Composite. Otherwise return I don't know.
Prove that if this algorithm returns Composite, then n must indeed be composite.
    Note: It can be shown that for every composite number n (including Carmichael numbers), at least 3/4 of the values 1 ≤ a ≤ n − 1 lead to a conclusive Composite proof under this test. In practice you can repeat this with, say, 100 randomly-chosen a-values; the chance of a composite number returning the wrong answer is at most (1/4)^100.


Problem 4. Pulverizer State Machine [15 points]

Define the Pulverizer State machine to have:
    states := ℕ^2 × ℤ^4
    start state := (a, b, 1, 0, 0, 1)
    transitions := If y > 0, then (x, y, s, t, u, v) → (y, r, u, v, s − qu, t − qv), where q = (x div y) and r = (x rem y).
Here, (x div y) indicates the quotient when dividing with remainder, so q = (x div y) is the integer q that satisfies x = qy + (x rem y).

(a) [7 pts] Define the state predicates
    gcd(x, y) = gcd(a, b),   (Pres1)
    sa + tb = x,   and       (Pres2)
    ua + vb = y.             (Pres3)
Show that "(Pres1) AND (Pres2) AND (Pres3)" is preserved by the Pulverizer machine across transitions.
(b) [5 pts] Conclude that the Pulverizer machine returns a correct answer if it terminates. Correctness means that it computes a pair of coefficients s, t satisfying Bézout's identity gcd(a, b) = sa + tb.
(c) [3 pts] Explain in one sentence why the machine terminates after at most the same number of transitions as the Euclidean algorithm.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on Problem 1, especially the induction in part (a). I don’t really see why that congruence should be true, and if possible I’d like a big-picture explanation before getting into the proof.
exchanges.tutor:
Good instinct to want the big picture first. Let's build that intuition together.

Before anything else: what are the base cases you'd need to verify for an induction on n? Try writing out what the congruence says for n = 0 and n = 1, and check whether they hold. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a big-picture intuition before diving into the proof. This is a reasonable starting point. Rather than explaining it to them, I should use the Socratic method to guide them to discover the intuition themselves. A good starting point is to ask them to verify the base cases manually, which will help them see the pattern and build intuition organically. I'll keep it bite-sized and let them lead the discovery.

exchanges.student: I get a little stuck even at that stage because I’m not sure whether regular induction or strong induction is supposed to be better here. For \(n=0\) I think it says \(F_a \equiv F_0F_{a+1} \pmod{F_a}\), so maybe \(F_a \equiv 0\), which is true, but then I start second-guessing everything.
exchanges.tutor:
Your n=0 check is exactly right — F_a ≡ 0 (mod F_a), and that matches F_0·F_{a+1} = 0. Good.

Now try n=1. What does the congruence say, and does it hold?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly verified n=0 and got the right answer, but is second-guessing themselves. I should affirm that n=0 is correct and then push them to check n=1 themselves. The choice between regular vs strong induction is actually something they can discover naturally once they see that the Fibonacci recurrence requires two previous terms - I shouldn't just tell them this. Let me keep it brief and push them forward.

exchanges.student: For \(n=1\), I think it becomes \(F_{a+1} \equiv F_1F_{a+1} \pmod{F_a}\), so \(F_{a+1} \equiv F_{a+1}\), which feels almost too trivial. That’s why I’m worried I’m missing the real idea—could you maybe just explain the whole induction setup in one pass?
exchanges.tutor:
Both base cases are correct — trivial-looking base cases are perfectly normal and not a sign you're missing something.

Now, for the inductive step: how would you express F_{n+a} in terms of earlier Fibonacci numbers? Start there, and think about how many previous values of the sequence you'll need.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly verified both base cases but is doubting themselves and asking me to just explain the whole induction. I should not do that - that would violate the 'never give the answer directly' rule. Instead, I should validate their correct work, reassure them that trivial base cases are normal, and then guide them toward the inductive step using a Socratic question. The key insight they need to discover is that the Fibonacci recurrence F_{n+a} = F_{n+a-1} + F_{n+a-2} is what drives the induction, and that they'll need two previous values (n-1 and n-2), which suggests strong induction. I'll nudge them toward the inductive step without explaining it.

exchanges.student: I know \(F_{n+a}=F_{n+a-1}+F_{n+a-2}\), but then I kind of want the entire proof template because I keep losing the thread on induction problems like this. That didn’t quite unlock it for me; can I see the overall shape without all the algebra first?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't lay out the template for you — working it out yourself is exactly how it clicks.

But you're one step away from the key move: you wrote F_{n+a} = F_{n+a-1} + F_{n+a-2}. If you're doing strong induction, what can you *assume* about F_{n+a-1} and F_{n+a-2} mod F_a? Write that out and see what happens when you substitute.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide the overall proof template, which would essentially be giving them the solution structure. I should not do this - it violates the rules about not providing complete or partial solutions. However, I can ask a targeted Socratic question that helps them see the next step naturally. The key insight is that if they apply the inductive hypothesis to F_{n+a-1} and F_{n+a-2}, they can substitute and simplify. I should push them to connect what they already wrote (the recurrence) with the inductive hypothesis they'd be assuming. I'll ask them a pointed question rather than giving a template.

exchanges.student: I guess I can assume \(F_{n+a-1}\equiv F_{n-1}F_{a+1}\pmod{F_a}\) and \(F_{n+a-2}\equiv F_{n-2}F_{a+1}\pmod{F_a}\), but then I’m not sure if that means strong induction is required or if regular induction somehow still works. I’m still pretty fuzzy on the difference here.
exchanges.tutor:
Look at what you just wrote: you needed *two* previous cases (n−1 and n−2). Regular induction only gives you n−1. That's your answer on which to use.

Now — you have F_{n+a-1} and F_{n+a-2} expressed mod F_a. Substitute those into the recurrence you wrote. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made great progress - they correctly stated the two inductive hypotheses needed. Now they're getting distracted by the question of strong vs regular induction. This is a minor conceptual detour. I should briefly address it but redirect them to the more important task: actually completing the substitution they just set up, which is the heart of the proof. The answer to their question is implicit in what they just wrote - they need two previous cases, which is exactly what strong induction gives you. I can make them see this themselves with one question.

exchanges.student:
I’d get something like
\[
F_{n+a}\equiv F_{n-1}F_{a+1}+F_{n-2}F_{a+1}\pmod{F_a},
\]
so
\[
F_{n+a}\equiv (F_{n-1}+F_{n-2})F_{a+1}\pmod{F_a}.
\]
I think that becomes \(F_nF_{a+1}\), but I’m still not seeing part (b) at all, and honestly I might need a more complete explanation of how the second induction is supposed to be chosen.
exchanges.tutor:
That's exactly right — you've got the core of part (a). Make sure you write that up as a complete proof (base cases + inductive step clearly stated).

For part (b): the problem itself gives you the key question — what are you inducting on? Think about what it means for b to be a multiple of a, and what values b can take.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has successfully completed the key step of part (a) - they derived the congruence correctly. I should affirm this clearly. Now they're jumping ahead to part (b) and asking for a more complete explanation of the second induction. I should not give that to them. Instead, I should first make sure they consolidate part (a) into a written proof attempt, and then ask a targeted Socratic question for part (b). The hint in the problem says 'what will you induct on?' - I should ask them that question directly rather than explaining it.

exchanges.student: I know a multiple of \(a\) looks like \(b=ka\), so I’m guessing I should induct on \(k\), but then I immediately lose how part (a) plugs in. I’m getting that “I sort of see it but not really” feeling, so I’d really like the big-picture connection for part (b).
exchanges.tutor:
Good — induction on k is exactly right.

Now try this: in part (a), you proved something about F_{n+a} mod F_a. What happens if you set n = ka in that result? What does it tell you about F_{ka+a}?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that they should induct on k. Now they're asking for the big-picture connection between part (a) and part (b). Rather than explaining it, I should ask them a targeted question: what does part (a) say when you plug in n = ka and think about F_{ka+a}? This will help them see the connection themselves. I should not give them the explanation.

exchanges.student:
If I set \(n=ka\), then I think part (a) gives
\[
F_{ka+a}\equiv F_{ka}F_{a+1}\pmod{F_a}.
\]
So if I already knew \(F_a\mid F_{ka}\), then the right side would be \(0 \pmod{F_a}\), and I’d get \(F_a\mid F_{(k+1)a}\). I think that’s the connection, but I’m still not confident I could turn it into a full proof cleanly.
exchanges.tutor:
That's exactly the inductive step — you've got it. 

Before you write it up: what's the base case for your induction on k? Once you have that, you have everything you need for a complete proof of part (b). Go write it up!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has essentially discovered the entire inductive argument for part (b). They correctly identified the base case implication and the inductive step. At this point, they have all the ingredients. I should affirm their reasoning strongly and push them to write it up rather than continuing to discuss it verbally. We've now spent several exchanges on the ideas - it's time for them to consolidate into written work. I should also briefly prompt them to think about the base case for this induction.

exchanges.student: I think the base case is \(k=0\) or maybe \(k=1\), and that’s where I start overthinking the indexing again. I’m close, but could you just confirm which base case is cleaner and why?
exchanges.tutor: Check both yourself: does F_a | F_{0·a}? Does F_a | F_{1·a}? One of those is immediate from the definitions. You'll see it in ten seconds — then commit to writing the proof.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to confirm which base case is cleaner. This is a minor detail they can figure out themselves by just checking both. I should not just give them the answer - instead, ask them to check what F_{0·a} and F_{1·a} are, and whether F_a divides them. This is a quick check they can do on their own. We've been going back and forth long enough - I should push them to commit to writing.

exchanges.student: I guess both work since \(F_{0}=0\) and \(F_a\mid F_a\), but I’m still weirdly unsure which one they’d expect. That angle didn’t really settle it for me, so I want to switch gears for a second: Problem 3 also feels murky, and I kind of just want a broad explanation of the whole Miller–Rabin logic before details.
exchanges.tutor:
You've correctly identified that both k=0 and k=1 work — either is fine, pick one and justify it briefly in your writeup.

Before switching to Problem 3: you now have everything for Problem 1, parts (a) and (b). Please write up your proofs first. Once you've done that, we can tackle Problem 3.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to switch to Problem 3 before finishing Problem 1. I should redirect them - they have essentially solved both parts of Problem 1 and just need to write it up. Jumping to another problem before consolidating is a bad habit I should discourage. I should also note that both k=0 and k=1 work as base cases, which they correctly identified, and push them to write up Problem 1 before moving on. I won't explain Miller-Rabin.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning throughout the conversation, never giving away the answer but instead prompting the student to derive each step. Base cases, inductive hypotheses, and the connection between parts (a) and (b) were all elicited from the student rather than stated by the tutor. The tutor appropriately declined to provide a 'proof template' when asked, scaffolded progression naturally, and redirected the student's attempt to switch topics at the end. No significant deductions were warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: Under 1.1, the tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never stated the key idea before the student derived it — each insight (base cases, strong vs. regular induction, the inductive step, the connection to part (b), the base case for part (b)) was drawn out through targeted questions. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently built on correct student progress (e.g., affirming the n=0 and n=1 checks, the inductive step derivation, and the k-induction step) and asked diagnostic questions before elaborating. Under 1.3, the tutor gave specific process-level feedback (e.g., explaining why strong induction is needed by pointing to the two-previous-case dependency). Dialogue quality was strong: no repetitive cycling occurred, the assignment was kept on track, and the off-topic switch to Problem 3 was promptly and appropriately redirected. Communication was concise and supportive throughout, with no monologues or tone issues.
